text: We are presenting the case of a sixty-two year old lady who presented with a symptomatic perineal hernia nineteen months after a laparoscopic abdominal perineal excision. A perineal attempt at repairing the hernia failed six months before this attempt.
We are presenting the case of a sixty-two year old lady who presented with a symptomatic perineal hernia nineteen months after a laparoscopic abdominal perineal excision.
A perineal attempt at repairing the hernia failed six months before this attempt.
We approached the patient robotically due to the fact that we would have to work deep in the lesser pelvis.
We approached the patient robotically due to the fact that we would have to work deep in the lesser pelvis.
The surgeon divides minimal adhesions, allowing visualization of the hernia defect located deep in the perineum.
The surgeon divides minimal adhesions, revealing the hernia defect located deep in the perineum.

The surgical team sets up the robotic ports similar to a lower anterior resection, with the patient positioned in Trendelenburg and slightly tilted to the right. The robotic system is docked parallel to the patient's left leg.
The surgical team sets up the robotic ports for the procedure, positioning the patient in Trendelenburg with a slight tilt to the right, similar to the setup for a robotic lower anterior resection.
The robotic system is docked parallel to the patient's left leg, positioning the robotic arms for optimal access to the surgical site.

The surgeon lifts the uterus and vagina to reveal the perineal defect located below the levator muscles, which is causing the patient's symptoms. The posterior part of the vagina is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon lifts the uterus and vagina, revealing the perineal defect located below the levator muscles, which is responsible for the patient's symptoms.
The posterior part of the vagina is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a levatorplasty using interrupted sutures to close the perineal defect, utilizing the levator plates for reinforcement.
The surgeon performs a levatorplasty using interrupted sutures to close the perineal defect, utilizing the levator plates for reinforcement.
The surgeon closes the small gap between the vagina and the levatorplasty by stitching the posterior wall of the vagina onto the levators.
Following the closure of the levatorplasty, a small gap is visible between the posterior part of the vagina and the repaired levator plates.
The surgeon uses the posterior wall of the vagina, previously stitched to the levators, to close the gap between the vagina and the levatorplasty.
The surgeon measures the perineal defect and cuts the Phasix ST mesh to the appropriate dimensions. The mesh is then introduced into the surgical field through the assistance port.
The surgeon measures the perineal defect below the levators, preparing to position the mesh for repair.
The surgeon cuts the mesh to the measured dimensions and introduces it into the surgical field through the assistance port.
The surgeon displays a Phasix ST mesh, which is prepared for placement in the surgical field.
The surgeon marks the site on the mesh that will lie adjacent to the bowel loops, using letters for identification. The mesh is then positioned precisely within the defect area.
The surgeon marks the site on the mesh that will lie adjacent to the bowel loops, using letters as advised by the mesh company.
The surgeon positions the Phasix ST mesh directly over the hernia defect, ensuring it is properly aligned with the marked site.
The surgeon prepares to suture the Phasix ST mesh using interrupted sutures at the pelvic brim, along the edge of the posterior vagina, and on the left and right pelvic sidewalls.
The surgeon plans to suture the Phasix ST mesh using interrupted sutures at the pelvic brim, the edge of the posterior vagina, and on the left and right pelvic sidewalls.

The reason we use the Phasix mesh is because of its characteristics as a bioabsorbable mesh. This mesh will be lying in direct contact with the small bowel loops and therefore we prefer to use a mesh in order to minimize the additions and the possibilities of fistulization to the small bowel.
The reason we use the Phasix mesh is because of its characteristics as a bioabsorbable mesh.
The Phasix ST mesh is positioned to lie over the defect, with markings visible on the side facing the bowel loops.
in direct contact with the small bowel loops and therefore we prefer to use a mesh in order to minimize the additions and the possibilities of fistulization to the small bowel.
The surgeon uses the robotic platform to place interrupted sutures in the lesser pelvis, carefully identifying and avoiding vessels in the area being stitched.
The surgeon uses the robotic platform to place interrupted sutures, securing the mesh within the defect in the lesser pelvis.
The surgeon uses interrupted sutures to secure the mesh, carefully identifying and avoiding possible vessels in the stitching area.
The surgeon identifies a pulsating vessel beneath the planned suture site and adjusts the approach to avoid stitching in that area.
The surgeon identifies a pulsating vessel beneath the planned stitching area and avoids placing sutures in that region to prevent vessel injury.

The surgeon stitches the Phasix mesh to the pelvic sidewalls, pelvic brim, and posterior wall of the vagina, covering the gap. After securing the mesh, water is applied to activate the bioabsorbable properties of the mesh.
The surgeon stitches the mesh to the sides of the pelvic sidewall, the pelvic brim, and the posterior wall of the vagina, precisely covering the gap.
The surgeon completes the fixation of the mesh and applies water to activate the bioabsorbable mesh, ensuring proper adherence and functionality.
This is a CT scan six months postoperatively where we can see that the herniation of the small bowel is no more evident.
